---

Oxidation reactions are usually exergonic reactions as they involve the loss of hydrogen atoms from energy-rich reduced compounds (containing many hydrogen atoms) to lower energy compounds. In the case of oxidation of food such as carbohydrates, some of the energy released through the breakage of bonds (i.e. removal of hydrogen atoms) is used to phosphorylate adenosine diphosphate (ADP) to ATP (i.e. *substrate phosphorylation*). The energy has therefore been transferred and stored in ATP molecules so that it can later be used to drive energy-consuming processes such as exercise.

---

## Definition

Oxidation reactions are reactions that involve the loss of hydrogen atoms (and their associated electrons) from energy-rich reduced compounds, yielding lower-energy products. They are usually exergonic, meaning they release free energy.

## Core Principles

- Reduced compounds are "energy-rich" because they contain many hydrogen atoms; removing those hydrogens (breaking C–H bonds) releases energy.
- The energy released by bond breakage during oxidation is not lost as heat alone — a portion is captured biochemically.
- In the oxidation of foodstuffs such as carbohydrates, part of the released energy is used to phosphorylate ADP to ATP, a process termed *substrate phosphorylation*.
- ATP serves as the transferred and stored form of this energy, allowing it to be later released to drive endergonic, energy-consuming processes.

## Sub-concepts

- **Substrate phosphorylation**: direct transfer of a phosphate group to ADP during the oxidation of a substrate, forming ATP.
- **Exergonic reaction**: a reaction that releases free energy, characteristic of biological oxidations of reduced fuels.
- **Reduced vs. oxidised compounds**: hydrogen-rich (reduced) substrates have higher chemical potential energy than their hydrogen-poor (oxidised) counterparts.

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

- Oxidation of carbohydrates (and other food fuels) provides the ATP used to power exercise and other energy-consuming physiological processes.
